speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 14, 1997. We're interviewing Micheline Kaplan Marmor. I'm Mark Gross. We're in Centereach, New York, and the interview will be conducted in English. Today is January 14, 1997. I'm Mark Gross. We're interviewing Micheline Kaplan Marmor. We're in Centereach, New York, and the interview will be conducted in English. Would you give me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Micheline Marmor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-R-M-O-R. And the first, too?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My first name is M-I-C-H-E-L-I-N-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Micheline Kaplan, K-A-P-L-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beaumont.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-A-U-M-O-N-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same, Micheline.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your place-- your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: August, 20, '23.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your place of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paris, France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I'm 73.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father is Charles Kaplan, same spelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Charles.

speaker: interviewer
text: C-H-A-R-L-E-S?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Estelle Kaplan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- your brother's and sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well, my brother is-- well, you want the name before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sammy, [FRENCH]. OK, [FRENCH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaplan. And my sister, Ellen-- Ellen Kaplan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Yeah, would you tell me about your-- your-- your house in-- in Paris-- what-- what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we lived in a very nice part of Paris, which is [FRENCH], [? Cannes ?] [FRENCH], which is, if you know Paris, it's-- a street between-- it's between actually [? Opera ?] and Madeleine. [? L'Opera ?] and L'Madeleine. Well, it's known, you know, known monument.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were born there. My parents lived-- as long as they were in France, they live all their life in this apartment. And-- and we have to move during the war, during the German occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the-- was it a large apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have two. The third floor was the business. My parents designed clothes-- high class, haute couture, we'll say-- clothes. And this was in the third floor. And then we live in the fifth floor. We have, you know, the-- the bedroom and the kitchen and everything. We-- we lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you have your own room? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did you share a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I share my room with my brother and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were the rooms large? Was it-- were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --large rooms in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, it was very large. But when I went back to France and look at it, it didn't look that large. Like the street-- I thought the street was a big street. And when I went back, it didn't look so big.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who-- who lived in the house? Who-- who-- [BACKROUND NOISE] Who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought I closed it. Well, my-- my mother and my father and-- and the three of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- and-- excuse me. And did you-- tell me about your friends. Did you have-- did you have a lot of-- a lot of friends there in Paris when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I went to school, you know, I had my school friend. And then after I went to study art, you know, in-- in the Beaux-Arts, which is like Cooper Union here-- the best school in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I have friend, yeah, I have friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about when you were growing up? Did you-- when you-- before-- before you went-- you went to school-- when you were in elementary school, did you have a lot of friends there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not that many, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not like here, that you meet in each other house. Everybody live privately.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to school during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you always go to school during the day. Actually, in France, you went to school-- I think you even went on Saturday. But you-- you are off on Thursday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when-- when you were off, on your day off of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm, that's a long time ago, you know? Well, I guess I'm home. And then I must have been reading or drawing or fighting with my brother and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you remember-- do you remember any of your-- any of your schoolmates back then, when-- when you were-- when you were going to elementary school, when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I really don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- I remember when I-- when I study-- well, that was-- when I study painting in the private-- when we say private teacher, there was a-- the-- the woman who study with me is-- was Chinese, or, well, she may have been Japanese. But she said she was Chinese. [LAUGHS] And I remember [INAUDIBLE]. And [INAUDIBLE]-- let me see. Yeah, that's all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And studying-- studying where? Where were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first I study, like I say, in the-- a private teacher. And then after, I study in the Ecole de Beaux-Arts.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm-- I'm--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interested in the-- in-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The feeling?

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the time prior to that, when you were-- when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --just-- just going to elementary school and-- and-- and that period of your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I don't remember that much, that period of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- do you remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your parents-- did they have people over to the house? Did you have a lot of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, you see, because in France-- we had the phone. But very few people had the phone. So then people will walk in without, you know, without calling. You don't do this here anymore. So there was always people coming and going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents had a lot of friends there at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my parents had. But they were-- they were very busy with their business. They didn't have that much time for free. They have, you know, they have that business, design clothes, which was very interesting because you have a lot of people working for them. They have the seamstress and the-- the tailor. And they have the model-- you know the model-- the people who wear clothes? And-- and they all-- the people who wear clothes have to speak English because a lot of our customer were American. And that was very, very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- and they-- they made these clothes, you-- you said, on the-- on the top floor of your house? That's where--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. the third--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's where the office was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the third floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: The third floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was the-- everything was dedicated to the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people would come in every day, then, to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: People would come and-- oh yes, people would come and work. And we have a lot of sewing machine. And it was a, you know, it-- [? naturally, ?] you call this?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, where they work-- you know, all the machinery-- and things--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like-- like a little--

speaker: interviewee
text: --like this, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a--

speaker: interviewee
text: And what was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a-- a sewing shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A sewing shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was interesting that every customer have there dummy. Some people were-- some of the customer-- they always come back. So some were kind of fat and everything. So it was built the way exactly the customer was built. And then my mother would say, give me Mrs. So-and-so, [LAUGHS] and I will bring the, you know, the dummy? Then they could cut the dress on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- did you help there in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did they have a lot of-- how many people were working up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all together, about 60. And we had the-- we had a man with-- actually, it was [? an Arab, ?] with the name Charles Kaplan. And what I didn't like, he used to walk me to school. And I didn't like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Just didn't like it. With a uniform-- yeah, I didn't like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you celebrate any Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parent were believe in God, but they were not religious. The only time we went is for Yom Kippur. You know, you play safe. On Yom Kippur. Maybe another holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were very working all the time. They loved their work. And they were working all the time. So I hardly knew-- I knew I was Jewish. But I hardly-- you know, I was not that aware because in France, you never talk about religion. It's not polite. You don't ask a person, what's your religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this country, religion is very important. You know, everybody has to belong to-- but not in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you have other family there besides your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we have a big family. My mother has three sister. Let's see-- one, two, three-- three sister and a brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did they get together? Was there family gatherings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, oh, yes, during the early day we have. And then my-- my grandfather and my grandmother. Yeah, during the early day, it was a big, big family. That's what I miss here, you know, because I don't have a big family here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did they get together-- did the-- did the family get to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for, like Passover, yeah, or if somebody got married, so we all meet. It was a lot, a lot of family because they all have children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- did they get together with the children and the-- the entire family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- very often, did they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for special occasion, not-- I told you, my parents were very busy [LAUGHS] working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to any of your cousins? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My sister more because she was older. Oh, yes, we were very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- did you have an opportunity to-- to play and do-- and do after-school and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I know we stay home. It's not like in this country, we go from one friend to another. More, everybody stay in their one family. I play with my brother and my sister is all.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't recall any-- any-- any other friends at-- at that time that-- that you can remember now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I have a special girlfriend, which we talk on the phone a lot. And, yeah, I forget I meet her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she'd have been Jewish, yes. And you know, it's very way. And I don't remember that much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have time to do any cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we have-- [LAUGHS] my parents-- actually, we are wealthy because of the business. So we have a cordon bleu cooking because, actually, all the model-- you call, like, the model-- people who put the-- show the clothes?-- they were eating at noon. So the cook was cooking-- was cooking for all these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: --on weekends, too, or just-- just during the week?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just during the week. But I remember is we have to deliver some clothes. And if it was not ready, they would work most of the night-- everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: That happen very often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime. [LAUGHS] But it was-- it was really an interesting life, anyway. You know, there's a lot of going-- coming and going.

speaker: interviewer
text: The customers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and a lot of them come from the United State and all over the world, they came. They-- they had a good reputation as the, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: The clothes--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- what-- what type of clothing was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: All type. My mother was doing the dress and the evening dress. And my father was doing the suit with, you know, there was a tailor there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was each article of clothing custom made for a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --particular person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything-- everything is custom-made just for one person. They got to choose the design and then the material. And this is the way it work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the clothing expensive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it must have been. But I-- you know, [LAUGHS] I don't remember. But I know it-- it must have been expensive, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you ever encounter any anti-Semitism that-- when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were growing up as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, some teacher were anti-Semite.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were saying-- I don't remember. But I know it was not comfortable because they-- they know we were Jewish. And they must have done some remark. I cannot say what, but I felt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- was your-- your brother and sister-- who-- who was the oldest in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was the oldest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my brother. My sister is three years older. And my brother is four years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have friends over to the house? Do-- do you-- do you recall? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, I think so. Yeah, they must have had friend. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was-- was the street like? Do you remember? Was it a-- was it a big street? Was it a wide street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I thought it was big till I came back 25 years later. It was a very nice street. And there was-- right in that street, there is a store called [FRENCH], which is very known, even in this country, where they serve very good food-- a special kind of food. And at the end, there was a Cinema Le Madeleine, a movie theater called Le Madeleine. And at the very other end of the street was a department store called [FRENCH]-- the very end. And not too far was [? Le ?] Gare Saint-Lazare-- Saint-Lazare Station.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were telling me, you live on the-- on the ground floor of the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not the ground floor. We live in the third floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: You live in the third floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was-- where was the-- the-- the clothing made?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the business-- the business was in the fifth floor-- the whole-- the whole-- you know, the whole fifth floor. It was a big house-- I mean, a big apartment house. And then the fifth floor was where we live-- eat and sleep. And then my parents got some customer who didn't want to walk-- especially American customer-- they didn't want to walk three floor. So my parents built the-- an elevator. It's still there when I-- [LAUGHS] that was my parents' doing. They built an elevator.

speaker: interviewer
text: To take people up to the fifth floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, the whole-- the whole house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the other floors in the building? Were they occupied, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- let's see-- third-- the second floor was somebody doing millinery-- doing hat. And the four-- the fourth floor some-- it was somebody doing-- how do you call corset, brassier, and all kind of thing like this. Yeah. And then we have what you call maid room. Maybe we had two maid room at the very top, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the sixth floor? Was there a sixth floor in the building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the sixth floor. I remember because it became important during the war, this-- this-- actually, when I stop-- well, we had maid. But not-- later on, we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ask my mother to let me use one room, that I make like a little studio to-- to paint and draw. How old were you then when you-- when you did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 16, about, 17. This part is not very clear, you know, because I didn't think about it. You didn't tell me you were going to ask me, [LAUGHS] so I didn't think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- tell me what-- do you-- you remember the-- the room, that you were in, though, and-- and when-- when you were-- when you were growing up and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I remember is every room has a fireplace, which is nice. And then we have-- oh, and the kitchen was-- well, in the-- yeah, across the-- you have the stair, and across were all the-- the material was-- you know, wool and silk. We had everything there. Later on, we use is as a kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you feel that you had plenty of living space, that-- that-- that there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --plenty of space for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. For-- for-- for France, it was a big-- big place. I know because during the war, you have to-- to light the, you know, the fire. Bring-- how do you call it? You know-- [FRENCH], and light the fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: And every room had a fireplace, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, every room had a fireplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how the-- the-- the building was-- was heated in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess most place must have been like this. I always like fireplaces. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did you notice that-- that-- that-- that things-- things changed, that-- in-- during the-- when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, you know, the war started in '39, I think. The declaration of war with France started in 1939. But nothing happened for a whole year. So we were living normally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was no change in your life at all--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. --during that period? No, we had no change because we-- we never dreamed that the German will, you know, will-- will come to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think in 19-- I forget when the German came to Paris. I have the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1940, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I have the-- anyway, when the German came to Paris, everything was still normal for almost a year. But they started-- they started very, very slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shall I-- shall I go on? Because I-- I wanted to look at-- What-- well, you say--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say when the-- when-- did you-- did you see German soldiers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, when they came to-- to Paris, we-- and actually, they were very proper, you know, they were very polite. They will give you-- they will give you your seat in the subway. And no problem in the beginning. They-- they were very, very nice, very proper-- in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what space did they occupy when-- when they came in? Did you-- did you know where they lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: [FRENCH] it's called. I really don't know where they lived. But I saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were-- they were all over-- all over Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were all over Paris. And- what was I doing then? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you were doing the first time you saw them? What they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, they just-- they-- they were all over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So can I look at the date at something? Not good, eh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, well, the date--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We can-- we can-- we can check the date. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Say--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the beginning was-- oh, first was the propaganda. After a while, they-- they started what you call propaganda. So they started to say horrible thing against the Jew. Like they will say, the Jew are dirty. They have greasy hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: They what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Greasy hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Greasy hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: Greasy hair. Hook nose. And all the problem in-- in France is-- everywhere in the world-- is because of the Jew. So in the beginning, people were, you know, they were-- didn't take it seriously. It was like a joke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was saying this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: The French radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, there was only the French radio there. So you listen. And-- but little by lit, people start to believe it. You know, when you repeat, repeat. And some people were glad to believe it, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was saying it on the French radio? Who-- who was-- do-- do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was in French. I don't know who say it. I guess the-- I don't if it-- it must have been the-- the collaborator, I guess. Is that the same one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the collaborator. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, well, it was a very, very slow process. They started by-- let's see when they started by. Yeah, all of a sudden, they say, every-- every Jew has to go to the police station and just register-- nothing else. And, you know, you always obey the law in France, you-- so we all went. If we have known-- [LAUGHS]. We all went and put our name. Then, after this, let's see, the next-- the next thing was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to the police station--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who-- who was-- who-- who took care of this? Who was-- who was in-- in charge of the-- of-- of doing this, of-- of recording your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: The French?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The French or the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the French, not the German. The-- the-- the-- the-- the French followed the-- the French law when they ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any German soldiers when you went to register? Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I really don't remember. You know, I was about-- I was 16, [? nigh. ?] So then the next thing was-- let me see. If I could do it [? connect-- ?] after this, it was-- let me see-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, well, you have to give up your-- your radio. You know, there was no television. You have to give up your radio. First, we kept it anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then-- too bad I could not [LAUGHS] refresh myself. You can take a look if you want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Let me see, just because I put it chronologically. Oh, yeah, something else. I knew police station, [? about. ?] OK, well, there's something else happened in my-- see, it's good if I can look at the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, certainly, you can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --look at it, if you wish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something else happened. All of a sudden, they put a-- an Aryan administrator. My parents work all their life to-- to build a beautiful business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they put, you know, Aryan in the same name, right? Aryan-- non-Jewish-- administrator. And my parents were not in charge anymore. That woman was-- who came, and then direct everything. Was it-- was that, a woman, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [FRENCH]

speaker: interviewer
text: French woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes. And then you have to write in front of the door, Jewish business in German-- I don't know it, forget-- that everybody knows that it was. So that was really disturbing. You know, the place didn't belong to you anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when-- when did this take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, let me tell you. I have to read. In April '41-- April '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when the-- they put--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- when they put somebody. OK? And then after, let's see. [FRENCH] The new law came in June, where you have to register in the police station.

speaker: interviewer
text: June of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: All this was '41. And then August 13-- you see how slowly it go? August 13 was confiscation of all radio. OK, the two last-- the two last laws was the-- the most difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're-- we're going to stop because we're at the end of the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK. Good. How do you--
